claimed_contribution: The paper introduces a multicriterion clusterwise linear regression formulation for joint segmentation that combines segment homogeneity and multiple dependent variables in one weighted objective.
It proposes and tests a simulated annealing heuristic for estimating efficient compromise partitions in this multicriterion setting.
It demonstrates the approach in a telecommunications customer-value application and discusses practical selection of objective weights and number of clusters.
context: The substantive application is a business-to-business telecommunications market-entry setting in which a local exchange provider segments potential long-distance accounts using overall satisfaction and switching likelihood. The empirical data come from a national telephone survey of telecom managers in moderate-to-large firms.
design_and_data: The authors formulate a scalar multicriterion optimization model that maximizes a weighted combination of explained variation for independent-variable homogeneity and explained variation for each dependent variable, with nonnegative weights summing to one. They solve it with a simulated annealing heuristic using nonnegative least squares for the clusterwise regressions and evaluate trade-offs in a Monte Carlo study of 16 synthetic data sets (a 2^4 design over true cluster count, number of independent variables, sample size, and cluster density) with two dependent variables. Performance is assessed using adjusted Rand indices against three true structures and compared with K-means and single-dependent-variable CLR via ANOVA. They also run an empirical analysis on 475 survey responses with five service-performance predictors and two dependent variables, testing K = 2 to 7 under multiple weighting schemes.
main_findings: In the synthetic experiments, the best multicriterion solutions produced significantly higher minimum adjusted Rand index values than K-means or single-dependent-variable CLR alternatives, indicating better trade-offs across conflicting true structures.

Weight choices materially changed recovery patterns: heavier homogeneity weighting improved recovery of the homogeneity structure, whereas heavier dependent-variable weighting tended to recover one dependent-variable structure at the expense of others under antagonistic structures.

In the telecom application, baseline single-criterion partitions had low agreement (pairwise adjusted Rand indices below .1), and the selected six-cluster multicriterion solution with v = .65, w1 = .175, w2 = .175 achieved omega^2(P) = .50, Phi1^2(P) = .65, and Phi2^2(P) = .64 with managerially interpretable segments.
one_line_summary: The paper develops a multicriterion clusterwise regression model and simulated annealing solution for joint segmentation, then shows in synthetic tests and a B2B telecom case that it can yield useful compromise clusters across homogeneity and multiple dependent-variable fit criteria.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior bicriterion segmentation work and classic CLR applications by addressing tricriterion-or-higher joint segmentation problems that combine homogeneity with multiple dependent-variable regression criteria and by examining trade-offs among conflicting cluster structures.
research_gap: Prior CLR implementations often did not preserve independent-variable homogeneity and gave limited attention to settings with multiple dependent variables. Prior multicriterion marketing applications were mainly bicriterion with a single dependent variable, leaving key modeling and trade-off issues for three-or-more criteria underaddressed.
research_question: How can a segmentation method jointly optimize segment homogeneity and clusterwise regression fit for multiple interrelated dependent variables in one partition? The paper also examines how to choose objective weights and number of clusters to obtain practical compromise solutions when the underlying criterion-specific cluster structures conflict.
